Title: Hadi: New opposition leader to be named before parliament convenes in June
Article:
KUALA NERUS: Pas president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang today said a new opposition leader will be appointed before parliament convenes in June.
The Marang member of parliament said the decision would be finalised following thorough discussions involving all opposition parties.
He added that there was sufficient time to reach a decision, as the Dewan Rakyat would not sit until June, with current priorities focused on resolving internal party matters and issues concerning Perikatan Nasional.
"This matter needs to be discussed with all parties because it involves all opposition MPs, not just one party.
"We will take into account the views of all political parties and opposition MPs, including those from Sabah and Sarawak. The candidate must receive the approval of all component parties," he told reporters here today.
Larut member of parliament Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin is the current opposition leader.
Meanwhile, Hadi reiterated his proposal for a special parliamentary sitting to address the global energy crisis affecting the national economy.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CMCF urges media, public to put dignity before clicks
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Communications and Multimedia Content Forum of Malaysia (CMCF) has urged media practitioners, content creators, and the public to exercise greater responsibility when reporting and sharing content involving road accidents.
The call follows several recent incidents that have drawn widespread public attention, including a fatal crash in Klang on Sunday, in which a motorcyclist was struck by a driver under the influence of alcohol and drugs.
CMCF chief executive officer Mediha Mahmood said videos of the accident have been widely circulated on social media.
She said while coverage of such incidents plays an important role in raising awareness and ensuring accountability, it must be handled with care as the visuals may cause distress or compromise the dignity of those affected.
"In situations involving loss of life, human dignity and compassion must come first. What is being shared is not merely content, but the lived reality of individuals and families facing profound loss," she said in a statement today.
Mediha said the CMCF recognises the critical role of the media in delivering timely and accurate information, but stressed this must be balanced with a clear sense of ethical responsibility.
She said the use of graphic or sensational visuals is often unnecessary.
"The reality is, while audiences may consume graphic content elsewhere, they trust institutions that demonstrate restraint.
"The media's credibility is also built on what they choose not to show," she said, adding that every victim must be treated with dignity, including after death.
Mediha urged media practitioners and content creators to avoid visuals that clearly depict victims and to refrain from disclosing identities without a justified editorial basis.
"In the face of tragedy, empathy must guide all reporting.
"This requires fact-based coverage delivered with restraint, avoiding sensationalism and speculation while remaining mindful of the emotional impact on victims' families and the public," she said.
She sid that such an approach supports balanced reporting and reduces the risk of causing further distress or misinterpretation.
Mediha also highlighted the growing role of the public in today's digital environment, noting that recordings captured at accident scenes are often shared without consideration of their ethical and human impact.
"The public is therefore urged to exercise restraint, avoid sharing graphic or sensitive content, consider the potential harm before posting, and prioritise assistance over recording or broadcasting incidents," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: French far-left MEP Rima Hassan to be tried in July over post on X
Article:
PARIS: Rima Hassan, a French far-left member of the European Parliament, will face trial in July over a comment she posted on X last month about a 1970s attack on an Israeli airport, the Paris prosecutor's office said late on Thursday.
Authorities arrested Hassan and detained her in custody for several hours on Thursday because they suspected the post made on March 26 and later erased could be construed as showing support for terrorism.
"At the end of her custody, Rima Hassan was given a summons to appear before the criminal court on July 7, 2026, to be tried on charges of advocating terrorism committed online," the office said in an emailed statement.
Hassan's message was related to the 1972 attack by the Japanese Red Army far-left militant group at Lod airport in Tel Aviv, resulting in 26 deaths.
In the post, Hassan had quoted on her X account a comment made by an individual who was convicted for the attack and sought to justify it by citing what he said was the oppression of people in the Palestinian territories.
The offence of online "terrorism apology" is punishable by up to a seven-year jail term and a fine of up to 100,000 euros (US$115,290).
The International League Against Racism and Anti-Semitism and the European Jewish Organization had lodged complaints against the post.
After her release, Hassan and her lawyer, Vincent Brengarth, said in separate posts on X that they would only comment on the process in a press conference on Friday afternoon.
Born in Syria, Hassan, 33, is of Palestinian descent and is a vocal pro-Palestinian activist and a fierce critic of Israel.
She was elected to the European Parliament in 2024 for the French far-left party France Unbowed.
Jean-Luc Melenchon, the party's founder, said the proceedings against Hassan were politically motivated.
Interior Minister Laurent Nunez dismissed the accusation.
"There are rules to be respected. Apologising for terrorism is a very serious offense," he said on TV station BFMTV.
The prosecutor's office has said that Hassan is the subject of six other investigations into possible hate speech while 16 others have been shelved.
The statement also said police found cannabidiol, or CBD, and a substance that seemed to be a designer drug 3-MMC in her possession. These will be treated separately.
Hassan has denied illegal drug possession, saying in her post that she takes CBD for medical reasons.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar junta chief elected as president
Article:
NAYPYIDAW: Myanmar's parliament elected junta chief Min Aung Hlaing as president on Friday, parliament said, with the ex-military commander set to maintain his rule in a civilian guise after snatching power by force five years ago.
The coup-leading general – who swept aside democracy in 2021, detaining elected figurehead Aung San Suu Kyi and dissolving her party – was anointed by pro-military MPs installed in a recent election overseen by the junta he leads.
The vote on Friday across the upper and lower houses of parliament in the capital Naypyidaw saw Min Aung Hlaing secure a huge margin over the second-place candidate in a three-person race.
"We hereby announce Senior General Min Aung Hlaing as president," parliament speaker Aung Lin Dwe announced from a stage in the parliament meeting hall.
He received 429 votes of 584 cast by MPs, a parliament official said after ballot counting was finished.
While the junta touted parliament's reopening last month as a return of power to the people, analysts describe it as civilian window dressing intended to launder the military's continuing rule.
The pro-military Union Solidarity and Development Party (USDP) won more than 80 per cent of parliamentary seats contested in the election which concluded in late January, while serving members of the armed forces occupy unelected seats making up a quarter of the total.
The massively popular Suu Kyi has been detained since the Feb 2021 coup, criticism or protest over the election was outlawed and voting was blocked in territories controlled by rebels which have risen up to challenge the military takeover in a grinding civil war.
With opposition factions still standing defiant after the poll, the conflict and ensuing humanitarian crisis show no sign of abating.
Tens of thousands have been killed on all sides since the coup.
Min Aung Hlaing is due to take power as president this month, while his two competitors – current Prime Minister Nyo Saw and Nan Ni Ni Aye, a regional MP from Karen state with the USDP party – will serve under him as vice-presidents.
In a post-coup period of emergency rule, Min Aung Hlaing served as both commander-in-chief of the armed forces and acting president, but to become permanent president he is constitutionally compelled to relinquish his military post.
He handed over the reins of the military to loyalist and former spymaster Ye Win Oo on Monday.
Myanmar's military has ruled the country for most of its post-independence history and presents itself as the only force guarding the restive country from rupture and ruin.
The generals loosened their grip for a decade-long democratic interlude beginning in 2011, allowing Suu Kyi to ascend as civilian leader and steer a spurt of reform as the nation opened up from its hermetic history.
After the Nobel Peace Prize laureate's party trounced the pro-military USDP with a landslide victory in 2020 elections, Min Aung Hlaing snatched back power making allegations of massive voter fraud.
Analysts say the claims were unfounded and he acted out of anxiety about the armed forces' waning influence.
Now that the USDP is entrenched in parliament with back-up from military MPs entitled to unelected seats under the constitution, the new government is expected to march in lockstep with the top brass.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Shamsul Azri: Many civil servants yet to fully grasp Malaysia Madani concept
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar today said many civil servants have yet to fully grasp the Malaysia Madani concept, which he described as more than a slogan, but a guiding ethos to be practised comprehensively.
While a large number of public officers have been exposed to Malaysia Madani initiatives, he said efforts must continue to ensure no one is left behind in understanding the concept introduced by Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim.
"Although I have been informed that 1,558,240 civil servants have received exposure to Malaysia Madani through various programmes, only 684,417 have completed the dedicated module via Epsa Intan.
"This exposure must continue. No one should be left behind. It is the responsibility of department heads to ensure every staff member understands the Prime Minister's aspirations," he said during the Selangor-level Jelajah Taat Setia Madani programme.
Shamsul Azri also urged heads of departments to reinvigorate communication with their staff, moving beyond a narrow focus on rewards or grades.
He said promotions should be based on performance, transparency, integrity and meritocracy, without favouritism or exclusion.
At the same event, Selangor Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Amirudin Shari said the Madani framework has become increasingly relevant as Malaysia faces new challenges, including a global energy crisis of a different nature.
He said that, unlike the Covid-19 pandemic, which disrupted demand, the current situation is driven more by supply constraints affecting markets.
Amirudin added that the Madani framework emphasises unity, stability, constitutional principles and national sovereignty as key pillars of progress.
"All these require collective effort to tackle the crisis that may affect people's livelihoods and the nation's sustainability now and in the future.
"This is precisely when we must safeguard unity and stability, as they will determine our success in navigating the crisis," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: PM opens first Madani Mart, aiming to ease cost of living
Article:
KUANTAN: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today opened the very first Madani Mart at Indera Mahkota 8, marking a milestone in the country's effort to make daily necessities more affordable.
The store is part of the Madani government's initiative to help ease the burden of rising living costs and support the well-being of families across the nation.
Spearheaded by Yayasan Madani, Madani Mart is a community store concept offering daily necessities at affordable prices and easily accessible to the public, under the slogan 'Kedai Runcit Harga Rahmah' (Rahmah grocery store).
At the launch, Anwar, who is also finance minister, spent about five minutes touring the store, accompanied by Deputy Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Minister Datuk Dr Fuziah Salleh.
Pahang Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Wan Rosdy Wan Ismail was also present.
Earlier, Fuziah said in her speech that Madani Mart is not merely a complementary initiative but a step to ensure the benefits of affordable prices are experienced more consistently, systematically, and closer to the public.
She added that the initiative also expands entrepreneurial opportunities within a more organised retail ecosystem.
"The target is to establish at least one Madani Mart in every state constituency, with 640 outlets nationwide within two years," she said.
She said the initiative is important to ensure wider accessibility of essential goods, including in rural and semi-urban areas.
"I am confident that this initiative will have a positive impact in helping to ease the cost-of-living burden for the people," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysian pharmacy group Big Caring files IPO draft prospectus
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The operator of Malaysia's largest pharmacy chain, Big Caring Group, is planning an initial public offering, a preliminary prospectus filed with the securities regulator showed.
Big Caring, backed by Kuala Lumpur-based private equity firm Creador, plans to offer a stake of up to 25.5 per cent, or as much as 1.88 billion shares, Thursday's filing showed.
Most of the shares will be sold to institutional and selected investors, with retail investors taking up the rest, it said, without stating the IPO price or timeline.
Listing proceeds will go to repay bank borrowings and set up a new distribution centre, the company added.
Founded in 2006, Big Caring runs 626 retail pharmacy stores nationwide and plans 50 more over the next three to five years, the prospectus showed.
Maybank Investment Bank and RHB Investment Bank have been appointed as joint principal advisers, joint global coordinators, joint bookrunners, joint managing underwriters and joint underwriters for the IPO, the draft showed.
AmInvestment Bank will serve as joint principal advisers, joint global coordinators, joint bookrunners and joint underwriters, while UBS will serve as joint global coordinators and joint bookrunners.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Australian drivers urged to refuel in cities as Mideast war deepens fuel crunch
Article:
SYDNEY: Australia's government urged motorists on Friday to fill their cars at city petrol stations if they're planning long road trips over the Easter holiday, as rural towns face fuel shortages.
Energy Minister Chris Bowen said hundreds of service stations had run out of diesel nationally and over a hundred were without unleaded petrol.
Panic buying since the start of the Mideast war has seen petrol prices soar half a world away including in Australia – that is largely reliant on imported fuel.
"For those Australians planning a road trip this weekend, given our shortages are predominantly rural and regional Australia, it makes sense to fill up in the city to help the country if you can," he told reporters in Sydney.
In a rare address to the nation Wednesday Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said petrol had seen historic price spikes and although motorists should enjoy Easter as they "hit the road", the months ahead may not be easy.
On Friday, the first day of the Easter holiday, 182 service stations in the most populated state New South Wales had run out of diesel and 48 had no petrol of any kind, Bowen said.
The diesel supply in New South Wales was being directed to farmers who needed to get crops in the ground to ensure Australia's food supply over coming weeks and months, he added.
In Victoria state, 76 service stations had no diesel and 37 had no unleaded petrol, with similar shortages across the nation.
Albanese's government this week cut taxes to lower petrol prices, but there had been a surge in demand at the pump by panicked motorists.
Australia's fuel predominantly comes from Asian countries including Singapore, Malaysia and South Korea, although Bowen said companies are diversifying supply chains to include the US and Mexico.
Bowen said 50 shiploads of fuel were on the way to Australia securing supplies for April and May, although there was a risk of shortages in June.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Media Council, industry players asked to discuss WFH police for media practitioners
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Communications Ministry has called on the Malaysian Media Council (MMM) to hold discussions with local media companies and organisations to gather feedback on the implementation of a work-from-home (WFH) policy for the sector.
Minister Datuk Fahmi Fadzil said the ministry's top priority in any decision on the policy is to safeguard the welfare of media personnel nationwide.
"I will also request the Department of Information to take the necessary measures, including gathering public feedback.
"Any further considerations will then be forwarded to the KPPA (Public Service director-general) and communicated to the Cabinet and the ministry," he told a press conference here today.
Earlier, Fahmi witnessed the signing of a memorandum of understanding between the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) and the Securities Commission Malaysia (SC) to strengthen enforcement cooperation in combating and preventing scams.
Also present were Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong, MCMC chairman Tan Sri Mohamad Salim Fateh Din and SC chairman Datuk Mohammad Faiz Azmi.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim on Wednesday announced that the work-from-home policy for the public sector and government-linked companies (GLCs) will take effect from April 15 as a proactive measure to address the global energy crisis.
The Finance Minister said the decision, reached at the Cabinet meeting, aims to reduce fuel consumption and ensure the sustainability of the country's energy supply, which has been affected by the conflict in West Asia.
Yesterday, the Public Service Department announced that federal civil servants stationed in Kuala Lumpur, Putrajaya as well as Selangor and all state capitals with a one-way commute exceeding eight kilometres are eligible to work from home beginning April 15.
"States observing weekly rest days on Sunday will implement WFH from Tuesday to Thursday, while states observing weekly rest days on Friday will implement WFH from Monday to Wednesday," it said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Thai PM blames stockpilers for fuel shortage
Article:
BANGKOK: Thailand's prime minister slammed on Friday oil traders for "excessive profiteering", blaming those stockpiling fuel or smuggling it abroad for shortages that have driven prices steadily higher.
"Authorities have found cases of fuel stockpiling and smuggling for sale in neighbouring countries," Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul said, adding the problem had cost the government 50 billion baht (US$1.4 billion).
"This action (smuggling and stockpiling) amounts to excessive profiteering from rising oil prices during the global energy crisis," he said, calling it "a major factor behind the nationwide fuel shortages seen in recent weeks."
Evidence of hoarding and profiteering among large and medium-sized oil traders had been found on land and at sea, he said.
Anutin said some seaborne shipments had been deliberately delayed so fuel would not arrive on schedule, allowing sellers to wait for retail price increases.
Other shipments had been diverted for stockpiling and were thought to be destined for neighbouring countries.
More than 57 million litres of fuel were reported missing during sea transportation in southern Thailand, Minister of Justice Rutthaphon Naowarat said.
The Department of Special Investigation is pursuing the matter as a special case and investigations are underway, according to the prime minister.
The allegations of stockpiling come amid public outcry over successive fuel price hikes in late March and early April.
Fuel prices in Thailand have reached record highs, with unleaded gasoline at 57.51 baht (US$1.76) per litre and diesel at 47.74 baht (US$1.46).
The war in the Middle East, triggered by US and Israeli strikes on Iran on Feb 28, has caused disruptions to oil and gas supplies and price fluctuations across much of Southeast Asia.
Vietnam also hiked fuel prices again this week, bringing the cost of diesel to 46,200 dong (US$1.8) per litre – up more than 140 per cent since late February.
The price of regular gasoline meanwhile was up more than 28 per cent since the start of the war to 25,970 dong (US$1) per litre.
Hanoi has dipped into an emergency fuel price stabilisation fund and scrapped environmental taxes to ease pressure on fuel prices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Hassan Marican appointed crisis management task force chairman
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Tan Sri Mohd Hassan Marican has been appointed chairman of the Crisis Management Task Force (PPPK) under the National Economic Action Council (MTEN) to coordinate government efforts to address the global energy crisis following the conflict in the Middle East.
Communications Minister Fahmi Fadzil said Hassan, who is also head of the special advisory body to the finance minister, will be responsible for reviewing daily actions that need to be taken and gathering feedback on West Asian issues.
"The PPPK has a secretariat chaired by the senior advisor for the economy in the Prime Minister's Office, Nurhisham Hussein.
"These two structures will assist MTEN, which currently meets every week, to coordinate information on a daily basis," he said.
Fahmi said this at a press conference after attending the exchange of a memorandum of understanding on cooperation in curbing scam activities between the Malaysian Communications and Multimedia Commission (MCMC) and the Securities Commission (SC) here today.
Fahmi also said the tasks of the team led by Hassan do not overlap with the special committee chaired by Finance Minister II Datuk Seri Amir Hamzah Azizan, which is responsible for examining current economic developments following the conflict.
"What is important is that our efforts are first and foremost regarding the supply, not only of fuel and energy for our country to remain secure for the coming months, but also the food supply.
"At the same time, we must ensure that prices of goods are controlled and activities of smuggling or diverting fuel and food can be curbed," he said.
Since Feb 28, Israel and the United States (US) have carried out military operations against Iran, resulting in thousands of casualties, according to Iranian authorities.
Teheran responded by launching missiles and drones towards Israel and US bases in several Arab countries.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Italy extends fuel excise tax cuts for month
Article:
ROME: Italy's government has extended by a month a temporary cut in fuel excise taxes that were due to expire on April 6, the economy minister said Friday.
"We are extending the reduction of excise duties already in force, which would have expired on Easter Monday, until the first of May," Economy Minister Giancarlo Giorgetti said following a meeting of the council of ministers.
He said the new decree-law -- to which some 500 million euros would be allotted -- also included a measure for agriculture businesses that would extend a 20 per cent tax credit.
After May 1, "international geopolitical developments, which are clearly beyond our control, will determine whether other types of measures may be needed in response to a situation that is objectively very complex from every point of view, including the economic one," he added.
Regarding the overall economic environment, Giorgetti said a discussion at the EU level over the 3 percent threshold under which member states must keep their deficit-to-GDP levels was "inevitable."
In the wake of Covid and the war in Ukraine, the EU has allowed member states to temporarily exceed the deficit limit during "exceptional circumstances".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang to study federal WFH guidelines before deciding on adoption
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: The Penang government will review and deliberate on the work-from-home (WFH) guidelines currently being developed by the federal government before deciding on their applicability at the state level.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the matter was raised during the recent menteris besar and chief ministers' meeting, which he attended together with the state secretary.
"The state government will refine and discuss the work-from-home guidelines to be implemented by the federal government.
"I attended the meeting yesterday, along with the State Secretary, and the matter will be brought to the State Executive Council (MMK) meeting next Wednesday," he told newsmen here today.
He said the current implementation target involved federal agencies only.
"However, if it is extended to the state and other agencies, it will be subjected to the acceptance of the guidelines," he added.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said recently a
WFH mandate
or ministries, government agencies, statutory bodies, and Government-Linked Companies (GLCs) would begin on April 15.
He said the measure was aimed at reducing fuel consumption to ensure sustainable energy use amid concerns over a prolonged conflict in the Middle East.
The government rolled out several measures to keep Malaysians informed about how it was addressing the economic fallout from the Middle East conflict, including the global energy crisis.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kerjaya Prospek wins RM98.78mil hospital contract
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kerjaya Prospek Group Bhd's unit, Kerjaya Prospek (M) Sdn Bhd, has secured a RM98.78 million contract from Sena Letrik (M) Sdn Bhd for building, mechanical and electrical, as well as external works for a proposed private hospital project in Negri Sembilan.
The contract covers the construction of a private hospital complex in Bandar Seremban Utama, Seremban 2, comprising an eight-storey hospital block with one basement level and supporting facilities.
"The contract will commence immediately and is scheduled for completion within 18 months," the group said in a filing with Bursa Malaysia today.
Kerjaya Prospek said the contract is expected to contribute positively to the group's earnings and net assets, while providing an additional revenue stream over the next year.
At the midday break, shares in the construction, property development and manufacturing company rose three sen to RM2.18.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Penang fixes school land tax at RM50 per lot
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: A total of 522 schools in Penang will now pay a fixed land tax of RM50 per lot, replacing previously inconsistent charges.
Chief Minister Chow Kon Yeow said the flat rate replaces earlier fees that ranged from RM1 to RM10, which often confused school administrators.
"Schools can now review their tax bills via the PgLand portal and complete payments online or at land office counters across the state," he said.
Chow described the move as part of a wider effort to improve administrative clarity and efficiency, urging school authorities to act promptly.
"Those who have already paid under the old system can visit the counter for recalculation. Any excess will be refunded or credited to their land accounts," he added.
The standardised rate is expected to simplify compliance and ensure a fair, transparent system for all eligible schools in Penang.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Over 150 global investors explore opportunities in JS-SEZ
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor has positioned itself as a leading regional investment destination after engaging more than 150 high-potential global investors in Shanghai, China, recently.
Menteri Besar Datuk Onn Hafiz Ghazi, who led the state delegation to Shanghai between April 1 and 5, said the investors' interest signalled growing confidence in the state's high-value opportunities through the Johor–Singapore Special Economic Zone (JS-SEZ).
The strategic engagement session, held in collaboration with China Construction Bank (CCB), brought together nearly 20 major corporations across sectors such as biotechnology, manufacturing, digital, energy, and aerospace.
Onn Hafiz emphasised the importance of attracting quality investments that can generate high-income employment, drive new technology transfer, and accelerate the state's economic growth.
"Johor's appeal as a gateway to Asean continues to strengthen," he said, adding that the state is ready to support investors seeking long-term, high-impact projects.
"We are committed to creating an enabling environment where international investors can thrive while delivering tangible benefits to the local community."
CCB, one of the world's largest banks with over 13,000 branches across China, played a pivotal role in facilitating discussions, leveraging its global financial network to connect investors with strategic opportunities in Johor.
Despite global uncertainties and geopolitical volatility, the session emphasised that international confidence in Johor remains robust, with investors increasingly viewing the state as a key entry point into Southeast Asia, Onn Hafiz said.
He added that the state government aims to translate investors' interest into concrete projects that will provide high-quality jobs, bolster innovation, and enhance the overall prosperity of Johor, ensuring that the benefits of foreign investment are felt directly by its citizens.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: BOJ keeps rate‑hike door open even as Iran war squeezes firms
Article:
TOKYO: The Bank of Japan will keep raising interest rates if its economic forecasts hold, a senior central bank official said, reinforcing a tightening bias even as fresh surveys show firms feeling the pinch of rising fuel costs linked to the Iran war.
While higher oil prices pose risks to economic growth, they could also push up underlying inflation by raising long-term inflation expectations, said Koji Nakamura, the BOJ's executive director overseeing monetary policy, in parliament on Friday.
The oil-driven pressure on underlying inflation might be bigger than in the past as companies are becoming more eager to push up prices and wages, he said.
"If our economic and price projections were to materialise, we will likely continue to raise interest rates," Nakamura said, adding that the degree and timing of future increases will depend on economic, price and financial conditions.
"We will reach an appropriate decision at each policy meeting by updating our economic, price projections and our views on risks using data available at the time," he added.
Nakamura's comments underline the BOJ's readiness to press ahead with moderate rate increases, even as fresh pressures build from outside Japan's borders.
Surging fuel costs and pricier imports driven by a weak yen are adding to inflation at home, complicating the central bank's delicate balancing act.
The message came alongside an increasingly hawkish communication from the BOJ in recent weeks - rhetoric that has pushed markets to price in about a 70 per cent chance of another rate hike as early as this month.
Yet the backdrop is fraught. Japan's heavy dependence on Middle Eastern fuel leaves its economy acutely exposed to energy shocks and supply disruptions stemming from the war.
Those strains are already filtering through to the corporate sector. Business sentiment worsened sharply in March, with industries from transportation and retail to machinery and chipmaking fretting about higher fuel costs, a survey by private think tank Teikoku Databank showed on Friday.
It marked the first time since September 2023 that sentiment deteriorated across all 10 sectors covered in the poll, which was conducted online between March 17 and 31 - weeks after the US-Israeli attacks on Iran on Feb 28.
The yen has also fallen over two per cent against the dollar since the war erupted.
"Surging crude oil prices have pushed up a broad range of input costs, while the flow of goods is slowing," a fertiliser maker was quoted as saying in the survey.
A separate private survey released on Friday painted an equally downbeat picture, showing service-sector growth slowing to a three-month low and confidence sinking to its weakest level since the 2020 pandemic.
While BOJ officials warn the war risks fanning inflation, some analysts say looming shortages of naphtha and other chemical products could pose an even greater threat - one that risks knocking a still-fragile economy off balance. The central bank may shed more light on how it weighs those competing risks in a quarterly regional report due on Monday.
The BOJ ended a decade-long, massive stimulus in 2024 and raised interest rates several times, including in December, when it took its short-term policy rate to a 30-year high of 0.75 per cent.
Governor Kazuo Ueda has made clear the door remains open to further rate hikes, so long as a modest economic recovery keeps inflation on track to durably hit the bank's two per cent target.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Baru–Singapore RTS Link hits 90pct completion, final phase underway
Article:
JOHOR BARU: The Johor Baru–Singapore Rapid Transit System (RTS) Link has surged past 90 per cent completion, transforming from bare concrete into a near-ready transit hub.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke led a rare media walkthrough of the Bukit Chagar Immigration, Customs and Quarantine (ICQ) complex, showing the project's shift from construction to operational readiness.
The raw structural frames seen in November have been replaced by a fully formed, brightly lit concourse, ready for operation except for ticketing gates.
At the immigration hall, e-gates are installed, and body scanners and baggage screening systems are being fitted, two months ahead of schedule.
The facility will feature 10 security lanes, 18 baggage scanners, and 100 e-gate lanes, including dedicated channels for passengers without luggage, to manage high volumes from day one.
[[nid:1409564]]
The 4km RTS Link from Bukit Chagar to Woodlands North in Singapore will carry up to 10,000 passengers per hour in each direction during peak times, with a journey of about five minutes.
With construction largely complete and systems being installed, the project remains on track for completion by the end of 2026, ahead of its operational launch on Jan 1, 2027.
At platform level, the station's signature façade is taking shape, and platform screen doors are installed on both sides. Remaining work focuses on communication systems and passenger information displays.
Outside, heavy machinery is being cleared for landscaping, road surfacing, and final access routes into the ICQ complex.
Loke said close coordination between Malaysia and Singapore has driven steady progress, with further engagement planned during his visit to Woodlands North Station later this month.
More than RM200 million is being spent on traffic dispersal and connectivity upgrades around Bukit Chagar, including new flyovers, expanded roads, and improved public bus services with over 250 additional buses.
A pedestrian link from JB Sentral to the RTS station is also planned, alongside upgrades to existing rail lines to support passenger services by mid-2026.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: RTS Link ticketing still being finalised, said Loke
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Ticketing details for the Johor Baru–Singapore RTS Link are still being finalised as the project enters its critical testing and commissioning phase, Transport Minister Anthony Loke said.
He said all projects faced challenges, but stressed that all appointed contractors are actively managing them, with no impact on the overall timeline.
The Light Rail Transit 3 (LRT3), linking Shah Alam in Selangor and surrounding areas, faced delays partly linked to Siemens' signalling package, particularly the late and unsatisfactory delivery of critical software needed to run the driverless system.
The setback disrupted system integration and delayed key testing phases, including fault-free trial runs required before operations can begin.
However, the issues were not isolated to a single contractor, with broader integration challenges across multiple systems also contributing to the overall
delay
.
However, Loke stressed that while both the LRT3 project and the RTS Link involve the same contractor, no issues related to Siemens have arisen in the RTS project.
"This is a very challenging project… there are issues from time to time, including integration challenges.
"But this is normal in any mega infrastructure project," he told a press conference after a project site visit at the Bukit Chagar Immigration, Customs and Quarantine (ICQ) complex here today.
"What is more important is that all parties, including Siemens, have given their commitment to take all necessary steps and resources to resolve the issues and ensure the project is completed on time."
Loke said there would be no compensation claims arising from these issues in the RTS Link project, dismissing suggestions of cost implications and reiterating that the project remains on track.
"As far as I'm concerned, there are no issues in terms of compensation. The project is progressing smoothly.
"Yes, there are challenges, but we are managing them," he said.
The project has now moved fully into the testing and commissioning phase, with physical construction largely completed and focus shifting to system installation, integration and daily trial runs.
Train testing has already begun and will continue in multiple phases through to the end of the year.
On ticketing, Loke said the fare structure and payment mechanisms are still being finalised, with RTS Operations Systems Sdn Bhd (RTSO) leading a market study to determine commercially viable pricing.
"The fare has not been decided yet. RTSO is conducting a market study, and it must ensure the system is financially sustainable," he said.
He added that any proposal for season passes would require careful consideration, including potential government support, but stressed that subsidies would be limited to citizens of each respective country.
"If there are season passes, we are only looking at supporting Malaysians. Likewise, Singapore will look after its own citizens.
"This is a joint venture, and the company must stand on its own financially."
Loke also said implementing a cross-border ticketing system is complex, as the RTS Link operates within an international zone involving both Malaysia and Singapore.
"This is not a domestic system. It is an international system involving two countries, so the ticketing and operational framework must be structured to meet cross-border requirements," he said.
Despite the technical hurdles, Loke said coordination between both governments and contractors remains strong, with open communication channels ensuring issues are addressed, given Malaysia and Singapore's strong bilateral ties.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Best offer or best only for Sunway?
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: As the deadline for the voluntary conditional takeover offer by Sunway Bhd for IJM Corp Bhd approaches on April 6, the tone fromthe former has grown increasingly firm - defending its RM3.15 per share offer as the "best offer" while clearly signalling that it will walk away if the bid is rejected.
Yet behind that firmness, some industry observers said it is becoming increasingly evident that the arguments presented are not only unconvincing but also raise deeper questions about the true foundations of the offer - whether in terms of valuation, structure or negotiation logic.
Sunway maintained that RM3.15 is its best and final offer, with no intention of increasing it. It also made it clear that it is prepared to walk away if the offer is not accepted.
"If that is truly an ultimatum, then the reality is simple - just walk away," an industry observer remarked.
In any corporate transaction, one fundamental truth remains: Sunway is the suitor, not IJM, the observer said.
"When the proposal has already been openly rejected by IJM's board of directors and major shareholders such as Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB), the position to apply pressure becomes inherently weak."
The logic is straightforward - if IJM were the one pursuing Sunway, there might be grounds to impose hard conditions.
But in the current situation, the suitor is the one exerting pressure on the target, the observer noted. That is not a sign of negotiation strength, but rather a reflection of an imbalanced narrative.
Sunway also argued that its performance has been far superior to IJM's, delivering stronger returns over the past decade.
However, observers said this argument raises a more fundamental question. If IJM is truly that weak, why would a corporate giant like Sunway still be interested in acquiring it?
In the corporate world, companies do not spend billions to acquire assets with little value. The more reasonable conclusion is that IJM is not weak but undervalued.
Using a narrative of weakness to justify a lower price is inconsistent, they added. More importantly, downplaying the performance of a target company during a "courtship" process raises ethical questions about the negotiation approach.
Strategic transactions should be grounded in recognising value, not diminishing it to force a lower price.
'Noise' Or Professional Opinion?
Sunway also urged government-linked investment institutions to evaluate the offer objectively and not be influenced by "noise."
But the definition of "noise" itself is problematic.
"Are market analysts, economists and research institutions to be dismissed as mere noise?" the observer asked. "If all independent views are considered irrelevant, then whose perspective should prevail?"
Surely not just the narrative of the bidding party.
Observers said in a mature capital market, diversity of views serves as an essential system of checks and balances. Without it, valuations risk becoming one-sided and potentially flawed.
As such, this argument may be interpreted as an attempt to sideline opinions that do not favour the bidder.
From a valuation standpoint, observers said the numbers do not support Sunway's case. Estimates suggest that the RM3.15 offer represents a significant discount to IJM's fair value, which is materially higher.
This makes the offer not merely unattractive, but potentially a transfer of value from IJM shareholders to Sunway.
In market terms, this is not a balanced negotiation - it is an attempt to acquire quality assets at a discounted price, they noted.
The structure of the offer also raises concerns. With only 10 per cent in cash and the remaining 90 per cent in Sunway shares, IJM shareholders are effectively being compelled to become investors in the acquiring entity at a valuation determined by the buyer.
Market risk is shifted onto IJM shareholders, while the upside is not necessarily shared equitably, they said, adding that this is not a balanced value-sharing arrangement, but one that clearly favors the acquirer.
More concerning is that Sunway only requires acceptance above 50 per cent to gain effective control of IJM.
This means control could be secured without a proper control premium, something rarely seen in transactions of this scale.
The implication is that minority shareholders risk being trapped in a company controlled by another party without adequate compensation, they said.
When Sunway describes this as its "best offer," it can also be interpreted differently: that the offer has been optimised for Sunway, not for IJM.
If the price were increased, returns for Sunway shareholders might be affected. As such, the current price may represent the optimal point but only from the buyer's perspective.
IJM Is Professionally Managed
Observers said ultimately, the market has already sent a clear signal.
The rejection by IJM's board, the firm stance by PNB and the views of independent advisers all indicate that the offer falls short of reasonable value expectations.
"It is also important to recognise that IJM is a professionally managed company, where strategic decisions are guided by shareholder value - not emotion or external pressure.
"In this context, any decision regarding Sunway's offer will continue to be driven by the interests of shareholders, not sentiment. And in this case, the offer clearly does not reflect that value.
"This is not about which company is stronger - Sunway or IJM. It is about whether the offer reflects IJM's true worth - and so far, the answer remains unconvincing," the observer said.
If the offer were truly a "no-brainer," it would not be facing layered resistance as it is today, he added. And if an ultimatum has become the final fallback, that alone may already suggest that the fundamentals of the offer are not as strong as claimed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sunview secures contract worth RM1.96bil from TNB Genco for renewable energy project
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Sunview Group Bhd's consortium, comprising its wholly-owned subsidiary, Fabulous Sunview Sdn Bhd, and Cypark Renewable Energy Sdn Bhd, has secured a contract from TNB Power Generation Sdn Bhd (TNB Genco) for a large-scale renewable energy project worth RM1.96 billion.
Sunview said the consortium, known as 'Consortium Cre-Sunview', received a letter of acceptance (LOA) today for the provision of engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning works for a 595 megawatt alternating current (MWac) floating solar photovoltaic plant, along with a battery energy storage system at Kenyir Lake, Terengganu.
Consortium Cre-Sunview is an unincorporated joint venture, in which Cypark Renewable Energy and Fabulous Sunview have 60 per cent and 40 per cent interests, respectively.
Meanwhile, TNB Genco is a wholly owned subsidiary of Tenaga Nasional Bhd.
The group said the contract value is inclusive of all applicable taxes, duties, fees, and sales and service tax (SST), with construction scheduled to commence on June 2, 2026, and completion targeted for Sept 29, 2028.
Barring any unforeseen circumstances, the contract is expected to contribute positively to the future earnings of Sunview and its subsidiaries.
The contract would not have any effect on the share capital and substantial shareholders' shareholdings of the group.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ramanan says gig workers now have 'channel for justice' to protect income
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The National Gig Economy Consultation Council held its inaugural meeting today, prioritising a minimum "floor price" for earnings to protect 1.64 million gig workers.
Human Resources Minister Datuk Seri R. Ramanan said the floor price sets a baseline income, ensuring workers earn a fair minimum while providing a safety net under the Gig Workers Act 2025.
The council, led by Datuk Dr. Mohd Ghazali, will immediately seek a "middle ground" for minimum income standards, he said.
"Datuk Dr. Mohd Ghazali will begin deliberations today. This will be a tripartite decision involving employers, contract entities, workers, and the government to ensure the best possible outcome for all stakeholders," Ramanan told a press conference.
Ramanan highlighted a fundamental shift in the legal status of gig workers, who were previously sidelined in the "informal sector."
For years, they lacked a formal channel to resolve disputes over pay or unfair treatment, he said.
"Before this, where did a gig worker go if there was a dispute? They had no avenue. But today, we have the Gig Workers Tribunal which will act as a mediator and facilitator. If negotiations fail, there is now a legal recourse. They finally have a channel for justice," he said.
The implementation follows the gazetting of the Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872), making Malaysia the first country in Asia to introduce comprehensive legal protections for the digital workforce.
While the Act directly affects 1.64 million workers, Ramanan said the social safety net extends to around five million people, including their families.
"We are creating a baseline where there was none. Previously, there was no midline for what a gig worker should earn or where they could turn during a rate dispute. We are now establishing that foundation," he explained.
To manage the transition, 22 Industrial Court Presidents and 28 industrial relations officers have been appointed.
Ramanan described them as a "brain trust" of seasoned industry pioneers and highly qualified academics.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 'I cannot grieve for long,' said widow of delivery rider killed in crash
Article:
SHAH ALAM: Refusing to remain in prolonged grief over the loss of her husband, Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, who was killed after being hit by a speeding driver last Sunday, Nor Nadia Abdul Majid is determined to rebuild her life for the sake of their young children.
Nor Nadia, 32, said that from now on, she would assume the role of head of the family and must earn a living to support them.
"I cannot grieve for too long. My children will grow up; they need my attention and education.
"So I must get back on my feet. I hope to fulfil my dream of starting a bundle (preloved clothing) business, which I once shared with my late husband.
"I want to sell via live sessions on social media. I need flexible work so I can always make time for my children," she told reporters at her residence at Pangsapuri Ken Rimba, Section 16, here today.
In the incident at about 11.40am last Sunday, Amirul Hafiz, an employee of TVH Malaysia Sdn Bhd, who was riding a motorcycle, was
killed
after being struck along Jalan Raya Barat in Klang.
Earlier, Social Security Organisation group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammed visited the family and handed over the Funeral Management Benefit (FPM) payment to Nor Nadia.
She planned to contribute under the Self-Employment Social Security Scheme (SKSPS), despite being self-employed.
"I intend to start contributing even as a housewife. At least when I am no longer around, my children will benefit from the contributions," she said.
When asked about her sons, Muhammad Aqif Amsyar, 9, and Muhammad Afif Izz Rayqal, 7, who are currently in Perak, Nor Nadia said they are still grieving for their late father and visit his grave daily.
"So far, both my children have yet to receive counselling.
"As for transferring their schools from Shah Alam to Perak, I will settle the matter next week," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Myanmar's Min Aung Hlaing: General who made himself president
Article:
HIS name was not on the ballot, neither did his photographs appear on campaign posters. But, one man loomed large over the general election held in Myanmar in December and January: junta chief Min Aung Hlaing.
On Friday, the 69-year-old general who has ruled the impoverished Southeast Asian nation since ousting Nobel laureate Aung San Suu Kyi's elected government in a 2021 coup, was elected president in a parliamentary vote.
The carefully-engineered transition came in the midst of a civil war triggered by the coup, which has displaced millions and left swathes of Myanmar's borderlands in rebel hands.
Min Aung Hlaing's shift from junta chief to the head of an administration with a civilian veneer comes after an election that analysts said was designed by the military to maintain the ruling generals' hold on power.
With Suu Kyi's party dissolved and other major opposition parties not contesting, the United Nations and Western rights groups deemed the elections neither free nor fair - and the polls were eventually swept by the military-backed Union Solidarity and Development Party.
Since the coup, Min Aung Hlaing has only had limited diplomatic contact with many of Myanmar's regional neighbours and has rarely spoken to non-state-controlled media.
Reuters interviewed six people familiar with him as well as two analysts of junta politics who offered insight into the thinking of the enigmatic general.
Myanmar's new president is a rigid military leader, but also a political creature with a fine-tuned sense for managing the country's elites, according to three of the people and the two analysts.
Those qualities, the people said, have helped him keep power through battlefield defeats that have dented the military's prestige and hold over the country, exposing Min Aung Hlaing to criticism from supporters of the armed forces.
Nearly 93,000 people have died in conflict since the coup, according to Armed Conflict Location & Event Data Project, a coalition of independent international researchers.
Pulling back from absolute rule and sharing power through elections functions as "an elite management strategy, diffusing responsibility and preserving regime cohesion," said Naing Min Khant, programme associate at the Institute for Strategy and Policy - Myanmar, a think-tank in Thailand.
"He became the leader not only because of military ruthlessness but because of his subtle skills that help reduce all sorts of pressure around him," said another of the people, a foreign former official who has met Min Aung Hlaing.
"I think if another person was put in that position, there may have been even more pressure on them."
Myanmar's information ministry did not reply to a request for comment on Reuters' findings.
Min Aung Hlaing has handed some generals lucrative positions atop military-linked businesses, even as he occasionally detained other senior officers, including court marshalling one likely successor.
Such moves have helped control potential rivals, according to Naing Min Khant.
"Power-sharing is managed through elite pacts embedded within the officer corps, where regime survival is closely tied to collective officer survival," the analyst said.
At the same time, Min Aung Hlaing has prioritised keeping important positions for loyalists, including some experienced at dealing with foreign leaders, two of the people said.
Diplomatic backing from China, in particular, has bolstered the general's position and supported the junta's recent limited comeback on some frontlines, Reuters reported in December.
Among the loyalists is retired military officer and former UN ambassador Than Swe, who serves as junta foreign minister, the people said.
One of them added that the diplomat has been coaching Min Aung Hlaing as he emerges from diplomatic isolation.
Than Swe has also since been part of efforts to rebuild diplomatic relationships with the Asean bloc that froze ties with the generals soon after the coup.
[[nid:1409540]]
Min Aung Hlaing's interest in politics was clear even before the coup, when he was serving as armed forces commander-in-chief, said another person familiar with the general.
A previous junta had pared back the military's outsized role in administering the country and handed power to a quasi-civilian government in the 2010s, but the general continued meeting community and religious leaders, the person said.
"All that didn't make sense, if you were only a professional soldier," they said.
The fourth of five siblings born to a family from Myanmar's south, Min Aung Hlaing read law at university in Yangon, then the country's capital.
In 1977, he passed out of the Defence Services Academy, the crucible of the officer corps and made a steady ascent through the ranks. This included time as a commander in Myanmar's historically restive borderlands.
The academy's motto - "The Triumphant Elites of the Future" - signals the institution's central role in shaping generations of military brass.
Most leave seeing the military as the self-appointed guardian of national unity, as well as of the rights of the majority Bamar ethnic group and the Buddhist religion many of them follow.
That sense of the generals as the country's ultimate protectors pushed Min Aung Hlaing to take absolute control in Feb 2021, months after a military-backed party was crushed at polls by Suu Kyi's National League for Democracy, said one of the officials familiar with his thinking.
"He felt justified in doing the coup," the official said. "Suu Kyi was not listening to him, to his concerns."
Suu Kyi, now 80, is serving a 27-year sentence for offences including incitement, corruption and election fraud. She denies the charges.
The politician has spent previous bouts of detention in the relative comfort of house arrest. This time, the junta has not released specifics on her whereabouts or well-being, though it insists she is in good health.
Malaysian Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim made a diplomatic push for the release of Suu Kyi last year, according to the foreign former official.
"Min Aung Hlaing quickly closed the door on that," the person said. "I know that this was their red line."
After casting his vote inside the heavily-guarded capital of Naypyitaw on Dec 28, a smiling Min Aung Hlaing walked up to a gaggle of reporters, where he was asked if he planned to become president following the polls.
"I can't simply say that I want to do this or that. I am not a leader of a political party," he said.
On Monday, Min Aung Hlaing stepped down as the chief of Myanmar's armed forces to seek the presidency, while also appointing a staunch loyalist - former spymaster Ye Win Oo - to succeed him as the commander of the military.
Under the military-drafted constitution, the president wields significant executive power but does not have authority over the armed forces - although analysts say Min Aung Hlaing's selection of a successor will likely enable him to keep a firm grip for now.
The next generation of military leaders isn't likely to take a significantly different approach toward Suu Kyi or the resistance movement, said Maj. Naung Yoe, who left the junta after the coup and now researches the civil war.
"There might be some who don't like the way the military is handling things and they don't like Min Aung Hlaing," he said.
"But that does not mean that they like the revolution."
* The writers are from Reuters
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: IGP: Media-police collaboration vital for public trust
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Strong relations between the media and the police are crucial for safeguarding national security and raising public awareness of the force's duties and responsibilities.
Inspector-General of Police Datuk Seri Mohd Khalid Ismail said the media, both domestically and internationally, can elevate the stature of the force, particularly in delivering services to the public.
"I believe that in the future, collaboration between the media and the police will not only enhance the image of the force but also help deliver timely news reports to the public, which are important for everyone to know," he said during a corporate visit to Media Prima Bhd today.
Earlier, Khalid, accompanied by Special Branch director Datuk Ibrahim Darus, toured the operations of Media Prima Audio (MPA) and the News and Current Affairs Department (CNA).
He was also given the opportunity to read excerpts of news reports for the Buletin Utama segment alongside TV3 news presenters.
Also present were MPB Group chairman Datuk Seri Dr Syed Hussian Aljunid; MPB Group managing director Datuk Mohd Rafiq Razali; MPB Group chief financial officer Rosli Sabarudin; Media Prima Audio chief executive officer Nazri Noran; Media Prima Omnia chief operating officer Datuk Mohd Efendi Omar; Media Prima news and current affairs division group managing editor Jasbant Singh and deputy group managing editor of news and current affairs and New Straits Times group editor Farrah Naz Abd Karim
The police's top brass included Integrity and Standard Compliance Department director Datuk Aida Abdul Hamid; Criminal Investigation Department director Datuk M Kumar; and Crime Prevention and Community Safety Department director Datuk Seri Mohd Yusri Hassan Basri.
Also present were Management director Datuk Seri Abdul Aziz Abdul Majid and Traffic Enforcement Investigation Department director Datuk Seri Muhammed Hasbullah Ali.
Commenting on Media Prima's news reporting, Khalid said he viewed TV3's coverage and other Media Prima network agencies' reports as extensive, providing both domestic and international audiences with accurate and timely updates.
"I believe the reports broadcast by the Media Prima Group come from credible sources and, in turn, foster public trust not only in the police but also in the Media Prima Group," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tougher penalties needed for fatal drink-driving cases, said Azalina
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Existing laws governing drink-driving offences must be reviewed and strengthened to serve as a more effective deterrent, especially in cases involving fatalities.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Law and Institutional Reform) Datuk Seri Azalina Othman Said said the current penalties for such offences remain disproportionately low compared to the gravity of the crimes.
"This is especially so when innocent lives are lost," she told reporters after delivering her speech at the Lawyering 2030 conference here today.
She said relevant ministries should re-examine the legal provisions concerning fatal accidents caused by driving under the influence.
"In terms of sentencing, the respective ministry needs to look at the Act.
"They can bring it to Parliament and request a commensurate amendment with heavier penalties.
"That is under their jurisdiction.
"In my view, Malaysians need to see heavy punishments before they feel trepidation.
"Otherwise, they might feel offenders can simply pay the fine.
"They might think a RM50,000 fine can be discounted to RM20,000," she said.
As someone with a legal background, she believes public awareness and compliance are sometimes only achieved through the implementation of severe and proportionate legal repercussions.
"Sometimes, the law needs to be amended so that the punishment clearly reflects the severity of the offence.
"If people see the fines are low, they feel it is something offenders can afford to pay," she said.
Under the Road Transport Act 1987, individuals convicted of driving under the influence of alcohol or drugs causing death face a prison term of between 10 and 15 years, and a fine ranging from RM50,000 to RM100,000.
Repeat offenders face even harsher penalties, with imprisonment of between 15 and 20 years and fines between RM100,000 and RM150,000.
However, Transport Minister Anthony Loke announced that the ministry is drafting further amendments to Section 44 of the Act.
The proposed changes would mandate offenders to
pay compensation
to the victim's family in addition to serving jail time.
The last amendment to the Act to increase penalties occurred in 2020, involving Sections 41 to 45.
The issue has returned to the spotlight following a recent case in Klang, where a 28-year-old lab assistant was charged with
murder
at the magistrate's court on Wednesday.
He is accused of causing the death of motorcyclist Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, while driving under the influence of alcohol and drugs on Sunday morning.
The accused was charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which carries the death penalty or imprisonment of between 30 and 40 years.
If not sentenced to death, the offender must also be punished with at least 12 strokes of the cane.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Anwar begins one-day working visit in Kuantan
Article:
KUANTAN: Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim today began his one-day working visit in Pahang by performing Friday prayers with the public at Saidina Umar Al-Khattab Mosque, Cenderawasih here.
His arrival at 1.15pm with Pahang Menteri Besar Datuk Seri Wan Rosdy Wan Ismail was welcomed by Youth and Sports Ministry Dr Mohammed Taufiq Johari, Deputy Economy Ministry Datuk Mohd Shahar Abdullah, and political secretary to the Prime Minister Datuk Ahmad Farhan Fauzi.
Also present was Chief Secretary to the Government Tan Sri Shamsul Azri Abu Bakar.
After Friday prayers, Anwar, who is also Finance Minister, is scheduled to visit Madani Mart in Indera Mahkota before attending Himpunan Anak Pahang Madani at UTC Kuantan, which is expected to gather more than 7,000 young people from across the state.
The prime minister will conclude his visit in the evening by officiating the 2026 PMX Pendekar Madani Cup Silat Championship at Pahang Sports Complex (Sukpa). — BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: T7 Global bags Berantai contract from Vestigo Petroleum
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: T7 Global Bhd has secured a contract from Vestigo Petroleum Sdn Bhd for offshore transportation and installation (T&I) works involving the FPSO Berantai.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia today, the group said the contract was awarded to its wholly owned subsidiary, Tanjung Offshore Services Sdn Bhd, via a letter of award dated March 18, 2026.
The company received Vestigo's approval on April 3 to disclose the award.
"The contract covers the provision of offshore transportation and installation campaign for FPSO Berantai," it said.
The contract is effective from March 18, 2026, for a duration of one year.
T7 Global said the award is expected to contribute to its earnings and net assets for the financial year ending Dec 31, 2026 and throughout the contract period.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Loke: RTS Link project on track despite Middle East tensions
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Despite the global geopolitical crisis stemming from conflicts in the Middle East, the Johor Baru–Singapore Rapid Transit System (RTS) Link remains on schedule, with contractors raising no concerns over cost escalations.
Transport Minister Anthony Loke said the RM5.24 billion project continues to progress as planned, with no requests submitted to revise contract values despite ongoing global economic and energy uncertainties.
He said physical works for the cross-border rail link are nearing completion, with the project now entering a critical phase involving system installation, integration, and testing ahead of its targeted completion by the end of this year.
"Structural and civil works are almost fully completed. We are now moving into system installation and integration, which are crucial stages before operations," he said after visiting the project site at the Bukit Chagar Immigration, Customs and Quarantine (ICQ) complex today.
Loke expressed confidence that the project cost would remain within its original estimate, noting the absence of claims from contractors for upward revisions.
He added that the project is expected to achieve Final Field Readiness (FFR) as early as September, paving the way for completion by year-end.
The RTS Link is slated to be fully operational on Jan 1, 2027.
Among recent milestones, installation of e-gates at the ICQ has been completed, while work on body scanners and baggage screening systems commenced two months ahead of schedule.
The facility will feature 10 security screening lanes, 18 baggage scanners, and 100 e-gate lanes, including dedicated routes for passengers without luggage.
Loke said close coordination between Malaysia and Singapore has been key in ensuring smooth project implementation, with both sides maintaining open communication channels to resolve issues promptly.
He is scheduled to visit Singapore later this month to meet his counterpart and inspect progress at Woodlands North Station.
In a related development, train testing has already begun and will continue in stages throughout the year leading up to full operations.
Once operational, the four-kilometre rail link is expected to carry up to 10,000 passengers per hour in each direction during peak periods, significantly easing congestion along the Johor Causeway.
The RTS Link will connect Bukit Chagar in Johor Baru to Woodlands North in Singapore, with a journey time of approximately five minutes.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Cypark unit wins RM1.96bil Kenyir solar project
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Cypark Resources Bhd has secured a RM1.96 billion engineering, procurement, construction and commissioning contract (EPCC) for a large-scale floating solar photovoltaic (PV) project with battery energy storage system at Kenyir Lake, Terengganu.
In a filing with Bursa Malaysia, the renewable energy firm said the contract was awarded by TNB Power Generation Sdn Bhd, a unit of Tenaga Nasional Bhd, to a consortium comprising its wholly-owned unit Cypark Renewable Energy Sdn Bhd (CRE) and Fabulous Sunview Sdn Bhd.
Cypark said CRE holds a 60 per cent stake in the unincorporated consortium, while Fabulous Sunview owns the remaining 40 per cent.
"The EPCC contract covers the engineering, procurement, construction, testing and commissioning of a 595 megawatt alternating current floating solar PV plant, together with a battery energy storage system," it said.
The project is scheduled to commence on June 2, with a completion period of 850 days.
"Upon completion in 2028, the Kenyir project is set to be the largest floating solar photovoltaic development in Southeast Asia, marking a major milestone for the region and reinforcing Malaysia's leadership in large-scale clean energy infrastructure," it added.
Cypark group managing director Datuk Ami Moris said the project underscores its strategic push to establish itself as a leading renewable energy platform, while marking the next phase of growth backed by a robust pipeline of quality projects.
He added that the development enhances earnings visibility and bolsters the company's prospects as it continues to translate opportunities into firm contracts.
"Beyond its commercial impact, it reflects our aspiration to be more than just a company by contributing meaningfully to nation building, strengthening Malaysia's energy security and accelerating its low-carbon transition.
"Moving forward, our focus remains on disciplined execution and delivering sustainable value to all stakeholders, while supporting Malaysia's transition towards a low-carbon future," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: High-speed chase through Ipoh neighbourhood ends after suspected cable thieves in BMW hit roadside pole
Article:
IPOH, April 3 — A high-speed pursuit through the streets of Bercham ended in a crash and the arrest of two men early this morning, with police discovering suspected drugs and cable theft equipment in their vehicle.
The incident unfolded at approximately 2:30 AM when a mobile patrol vehicle unit on routine patrol spotted a vehicle behaving suspiciously.
When ordered to pull over, the driver sped off, triggering a chase through the neighborhood.
Ipoh district police chief ACP Najib Hamzah said the suspects drove dangerously before losing control and colliding with a roadside pole.
“With the assistance of several police units, the vehicle was eventually intercepted, and both suspects were successfully apprehended,” he said in a statement today.
A subsequent search of the vehicle uncovered a quantity of substances believed to be methamphetamine (syabu), along with various tools typically used for cable theft.
The suspects, aged between 18 and 30, have been remanded to assist with investigations.
The case is being investigated under relevant drug laws and for the possession of equipment intended for criminal use.

Title: Socso to provide RM2,888 monthly lifeline to Klang rider’s widow and three kids
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 3 — The welfare of the widow and three children of a motorcyclist, killed in a road accident at Jalan Raya Barat, Klang, last Sunday, will continue to be safeguarded, as they qualify for a monthly Survivors’ Pension of RM2,888.17 from the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso).
Perkeso Group chief executive officer Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammed said checks found that the late Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, who had worked at TVH Malaysia Sdn Bhd for 15 years, was an active contributor under the Employees’ Social Security Act 1969 (Act 4).
“The late Amirul Hafiz’s records meet the eligibility requirements under the Invalidity Scheme, thus qualifying his dependants to receive the Survivors’ Pension as a form of continuous financial support.
“His contributions now serve as a lifeline for his wife, Nor Nadia Abdul Majid, 32, and their three children, Muhammad Aqif Amsyar, Muhammad Afif Izz Rayqal, and Nur Humaira, aged between two and nine,” he said.
He said this when visiting and presenting the Survivors’ Pension and Funeral Management Benefit amounting to RM3,000 to Nor Nadia at Ken Rimba Apartments, Section 16, here, today.
Mohammed Azman said that Nor Nadia is eligible for monthly payments for life, while the children will receive financial assistance for their education until the age of 21, or upon completion of their first degree, with the total monthly benefit being RM2,888.17.
“The loss of a loved one cannot be replaced, but the social protection provided by Perkeso can serve as an important safeguard in ensuring the continued livelihood of the dependants.
“Consistent contributions are the foundation of this protection, and in this case, they have become a lifeline for the future of the deceased’s family,” he said.
Mohammed Azman also urged employers who have yet to register to do so immediately, and contribute for their employees under the Employees’ Social Security Act 1969, as the protection provided today serves as a guarantee for the future, given that misfortune can strike at any time.
He added that social protection in the informal sector has also been strengthened through the enforcement of the Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872), which came into force on March 31.
“This new Act officially provides social security protection under Perkeso to more than 1.64 million gig workers in Malaysia, ensuring compensation and welfare if they are involved in accidents while working,” he said.
Last Wednesday (April 1), a laboratory assistant, R. Saktygaanapathy, 28, was charged at the Klang Magistrate’s Court with the murder of Amirul Hafiz.
Previously, Klang Selatan police chief ACP Lim Jit Huey reportedly said that a motorcyclist was killed after being hit by a car driver, believed to be under the influence of alcohol and drugs, in an incident at about 11.40 am on Sunday (March 29).
In another development, Mohammed Azman said there are employers in Malaysia who do not consistently contribute to Perkeso in order to cut costs.
He said that this was detected through record checks, before Perkeso took action against these errant employers. — Bernama

Title: After demanding a ‘life for a life’, Klang rider’s widow seeks tougher laws to end drugged driving
Article:
SHAH ALAM, April 3 — The widow of a man who died after being hit by a driver under the influence of drugs in Klang recently hopes the government will consider amending existing penalties and laws to prevent similar incidents from recurring.
Nor Nadia Abdul Majid, 32, the wife of the late Amirul Hafiz Omar, 33, said she hopes her husband’s death would serve as a lesson to society and lead to changes in current legislation.
“There may be wisdom behind my husband’s passing. God willing, I pray that there will be changes in terms of penalties and the law,” she said when met by reporters at her residence at Ken Rimba Apartments, Section 16, here today.
Earlier, she received a visit from Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) Group chief executive Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohammed, who presented a Survivors’
Pension as well as Funeral Benefit amounting to RM3,000.
The mother of three said contributions from the public of various backgrounds have helped ease the emotional burden and support her family’s livelihood.
Nor Nadia said she is now striving to move forward for the sake of her young children and hopes to realise her dream of opening a second-hand clothing or bundle shop.
“Many have contributed that I even asked them to just leave it outside. Malaysians are truly caring. I must rise and cannot continue to grieve,” she said.
She said her children still miss their late father and frequently visit his grave, while matters related to their school transfer are expected to be resolved next week.
Last Wednesday, a 28-year-old laboratory assistant was charged at the Klang Magistrate’s Court with the murder of her husband last Sunday.
R. Saktygaanapathy, 28, was charged under Section 302 of the Penal Code, which carries the death penalty or imprisonment of up to 40 years and, if not sentenced to death, a minimum of 12 strokes of the cane upon conviction.
In another charge at the same court, he pleaded guilty to self-administration of drugs believed to be benzodiazepine (Benzo) and tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), a charge under Section 15(1)(a) of the Dangerous Drugs Act 1952, which provides for a fine of up to RM5,000, imprisonment of up to two years, or both, and may include a supervision order of up to three years upon conviction. — Bernama

Title: Hadi: New Opposition leader to be named before June Parliament meeting
Article:
KUALA NERUS, 3 April — A new Opposition Leader will be finalised before the Dewan Rakyat reconvenes in June, PAS president Tan Sri Abdul Hadi Awang said today.
While a candidate has yet to be chosen, the Marang MP said that the decision would only be reached after exhaustive discussions involving all opposition parties to ensure a unified front,
Bernama
reported.
Hadi noted that there is no immediate rush, as the next parliamentary sitting is months away, asserting that the coalition’s current priorities are focused on internal party matters and broader issues within Perikatan Nasional (PN).
“This matter requires consensus because it involves all opposition MPs, not just a single party,” Hadi told reporters at an event here today.
“We will take into account the views of all political parties and opposition lawmakers, including those from Sabah and Sarawak. The final candidate must receive the unanimous approval of all component parties.”
The search for a new leader follows the political uncertainty surrounding the current Opposition leader, Datuk Seri Hamzah Zainudin, who has remained in the role despite his sacking from Bersatu in February.
Under parliamentary convention, the Opposition leader is typically the head of the largest opposition bloc.

Title: RTS Link on track for Jan 2027 opening; costs stable despite global energy crisis
Article:
JOHOR BAHRU, April 3 — The global energy crisis triggered by the conflict in the Middle East has not impacted the progress of the Johor Bahru-Singapore Rapid Transit System Link (RTS Link), Transport Minister Anthony Loke confirmed today.
The minister said that the project remains on schedule, with no requests for cost revisions from contractors despite prevailing economic uncertainties.
“So far, I am confident that costs remain within original estimates. The RTS Link is entering a critical phase this year ahead of its launch in January 2027,” Loke told reporters following a briefing at the Bukit Chagar Customs, Immigration and Quarantine (CIQ) complex.
He noted that physical construction is nearly finished, and the project has transitioned to system installation and integration testing. These phases are expected to be completed by the end of 2026.
